2026 World Cup Fantasy Beginner’s Guide: Rules, Tips, and More
Welcome to our Beginner’s guide to FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026!
In this post, we’ll be outlining the key rules of WC Fantasy and how it may differ from other popular fantasy games (such as FPL), and, at the end of the article, explaining how to use some of these rules to your advantage…
Over the next few weeks, we’ll be posting a whole host of content focused on FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026, from
how-to guides
like this to
differentials
,
players to target,
and more! With the World Cup only two weeks away, now is as good a time as any to get drafting, planning, and scheming for MD1 (11/06)…
FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026 – Overview
FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026 shares many similarities with FPL. The basic rules remain pretty much the same…
Similarities
Pick a Squad of 15 Players (Initially $100m budget)
Captain scores double points
Chips (or boosters as they are known in WC Fantasy) are available
Points scored for goals, assists, clean sheets, and more…
Unlike FPL, however, there are a few key differences which can make a big difference…
Differences
Substitutions and Captaincy changes can be made mid-matchday – the deadline for such changes comes at the end of the MD rather than the beginning, like in FPL.
Players will not rise and fall in price as they do in FPL.
Chips (Boosters) are different
The budget and team limits change after the group stage (budget increases, and you can select more and more players per team as the tournament progresses)
Points scoring is a bit different (though the basics remain the same), e.g., bonus points for differentials, Shots on target, Chances created, Tackles…
Let’s explore all of this in a bit more detail…
2026 World Cup Fantasy – Initial Squad Selection
To begin with, one must select a squad of 15 players (two goalkeepers, five defenders, five midfielders, and three forwards). Managers have an
initial $100m budget
to play with and are
limited to three players maximum from each nation
throughout the group stage. Players range from
$10.5m to $3.5m, and prices are fixed,
meaning there will be no price rises/falls at all across the tournament. Unlimited free changes (transfers and substitutions) can be made to this squad prior to the MD1 deadline on Thursday, 11th June.
Substitutions and Captaincy
Substitutions
can be made as normal prior to the start of a MD as is standard practice in FPL, however they can also be made under a specific set of circumstances while the Match Day is ‘live’ (after the start of the first match of the matchday but before the end of the final match of the round).
Manual Substitutions
“You can replace a player from your starting 11 with someone from your bench, as long as the bench player hasn’t played yet and your new lineup still follows a valid formation.”
A player that has already played that MD cannot be substituted into the starting XI.
Players that are currently playing (‘live’ players) cannot be substituted into or out of the starting XI.
You can swap two players that are yet to play into and out of your starting XI as many times as you want.
Auto-Subs
ALTERNATIVELY: As in FPL, if one of your starting players does not play, an
auto-sub will occur.
This means that a player on your bench (prioritised 1-3 as ordered on your bench) will come on for the non-playing starter.
It must be noted that
auto-subs will only occur if you have NOT made manual substitutions
. In other words, by making manual substitutions, auto-subs will not occur at all.
Captaincy
Captaincy (and VC) can be changed an unlimited number of times prior to the start of a matchday (as in FPL), but captaincy changes can also be made under a certain set of circumstances while the MatchDay is ‘live’.
Simply put,
the same circumstances that apply to making manual substitutions also apply to captaincy
in Fifa World Cup Fantasy 2026…
You can swap captaincy from a player whose team has completed their match to a player who has not yet played.
You cannot swap the captaincy onto a player who has already completed their match (obviously).
You can only put the captaincy on a player in your playing XI (as in FPL).
PLEASE NOTE: THESE RULES DO NOT CHANGE ACROSS THE TOURNAMENT

to join our WC Fantasy Mini League (Code: S8DIP9RE)
World Cup Fantasy Beginner’s Guide – Transfers
The basics of transfers in Fifa World Cup Fantasy 2026
work the same as they do in FPL.
You can ‘transfer out’ one or multiple players in your team for other players in the WC, so long as you remain ‘in budget’.
Unlimited transfers can be made to your team prior to the MD1 deadline
(11/06). The team player limits, budget, and number of free transfers fluctuate as the tournament progresses…
Group stage (MD1-MD3):
2 Free Transfers per MD (-3 points for any additional transfers)
$100m budget (no price changes)
Max 3 players per country
Round of 32:
Unlimited Transfers (no penalties)
$105m budget (no price changes)
Max 3 players per country
Round of 16:
4 Free Transfers (-3 for any additional transfers)
$105m budget (no price changes)
Max 4 players per country
Quarter Finals:
4 Free Transfers (-3 for any additional transfers)
$105m budget (no price changes)
Max 5 players per country
Semi Finals:
5 Free Transfers (-3 for any additional transfers)
$105m budget (no price changes)
Max 6 players per country
Final:
6 Free Transfers (-3 for any additional transfers)
$105m budget (no price changes)
Max 8 players per country
While these rules may seem daunting, all you really need to remember is that free transfers, player nation limits,
and the budget increases across the tournament as more and more countries are eliminated
. It will be pretty clear within the game what these limits are on any given matchday.
World Cup Fantasy Beginner’s Guide – Points Scoring
The part you’ve all been waiting for – how to actually score points in FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026!
All players:
Appearance (up to 60 minutes): +1
Appearance (60+ mins): +1
Assist: +3
Winning a penalty: +2
Yellow card: -1
Red card: -2
Own goal: -2
Conceding a penalty: -1
Goalkeepers:
Clean sheet (60+ mins): +5
First goal conceded: 0
Each additional goal conceded: -1
Goal scored: +9
Penalty save (not including shootouts): +3
Every three saves: +1
Defenders:
Clean sheet (60+ mins): +5
First goal conceded: 0
Each additional goal conceded: -1
Goal scored: +7
Midfielders
Clean sheet (60+ mins): +1
Goal scored: +6
Every three tackles: +1
Every two chances created: +1
Forwards
Goal scored: +5
Every two shots on target: +1
Bonus points
Goal from direct free-kick: +1 (in addition to the basic goal points)
Scouting bonus: +2 *
As you can see, most of the rules remain the same, however there are some key differences:
Key Rule Changes:
Points for chances created and shots on target
will come as a welcome change from FPL, which only rewards goal involvements
Defcon vs. tackles
– only midfielders get points for tackles (every 3)
Bonus point for free-kick goals
Scouting bonus
: If any of your players score more than 4 points in a match and are in fewer than 5% of all teams’ selection.
2026 World Cup Fantasy Chips / Boosters
Wildcard
: Unlimited transfers (cannot be used in RO32)
12th man
: Select a player from your bench to score points for your team in a given round (bench boost, but just for one player)
Maximum Captaincy
: The player who scores the most points in your starting XI in a given round is automatically assigned the captaincy.
Qualification Booster
: +2 points for any player in your team that progresses to the next round of the tournament or wins the final (only available from Round of 32 onwards).

Best Defenders to Target in FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026
Welcome to our guide to the
Best Defenders to Target in FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026!
In this post, we’ll be taking a look at some of the key defensive assets to target in FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026 by analysing the fixtures, previous performances in international and domestic competition, as well as the point scoring system for defenders in Fantasy WC.
In this post, we’ll mostly be looking at players to target in the group stage, as all managers will receive unlimited transfers between the end of the group stage and the start of the Round of 32. Before we run through some of the potential options for our MD1 teams, let us first go over how defenders score points in FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026…

Point Scoring in FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026 – Defenders
Appearance (up to 60 minutes): +1
Appearance (60+ minutes): +1
Clean sheet: +5
Goal Scored: +7
Assist: +3
Scouting Bonus: +2
There are other bonuses for scoring free kicks or winning penalties, as well as the standard subtractions for yellow and red cards, own goals, and conceding goals/penalties. Based on the way that defenders score points in FPL, it would appear that targeting
lower ownership defenders that
are likely to secure CS (and therefore maximising the scouting bonus) rather than searching out more popular ‘attacking’ defenders could be a good way to consistent point scoring.

Best Defenders to Target in FIFA World Cup Fantasy 2026
Konsa ($4.8m – England)
England are certainly a defense to target for this upcoming tournament, considering their clean sheet form in the play-offs. England didn’t concede a single goal across their 8 qualification games, and Konsa played every single minute. It’s more than likely that Guehi and Konsa will be England’s starting defensive partnership, and as Konsa comes in $0.3m lower than Guehi, it makes sense to target him going into MD1.
England’s MD1 fixture is far from easy (Croatia) but is certainly winnable and definitely offers some CS potential (considering England’s recent defensive record). Furthermore,
after MD1, England have two ‘easier’ fixtures against Ghana and Panama
. At just $4.8m Konsa could certainly be rotated out of your team for the MD1 fixture in favour of a player whose team is playing earlier on in the matchday (England play in the third from final fixture of MD1).
Konsa is currently just 1.1% selected
(though this will likely rise prior to the tournament), meaning that he may well qualify for the ‘scouting bonus’ points (+2 points for players that score 4+ points in a matchday and are less than 5% owned).
A CS gets your defenders 5 points in World Cup fantasy
, so just by getting a CS Konsa could get close to a double-digit haul (2 points for playing 60+, 5 points for CS, 2 points for scouting bonus = 9 points) if his ownership stays low.
With England’s fullback situation still a bit up in the air and Konsa coming in lower than the other ‘nailed’ England assets, he could be a gem, particularly if his ownership stays low!
If we get more clarity on the fullback situation going into MD1, then the likes of James could become interesting propositions, but until that happens,
the only double up I would consider would be Konsa/Guehi + Pickford.
Further Read:
World Cup Fantasy: Groups A-D: Best Teams & Players to Target
Cucurella ($5.1m – Spain)
Spain have two good fixtures to start their WC26 journey, facing Cape Verde and Saudi Arabia
in MD1 and MD2 before playing Uruguay in MD3, making their defenders very attractive assets going into MD1.
The betting markets have Spain with the highest CS potential across MD1 and MD2
, backing up their defensive solidity shown across their qualification journey (conceded just 2 goals in their 6 qualification games last year).
If we take a look at Spain’s performance in their most recent international tournament, they were very solid defensively,
conceding ZERO goals across their 3 group stage games
and then conceding 1 goal in each of their knockout matches. Spain were a bit lucky to concede 0 across the group stage, conceding a lot of chances against Croatia and 10 shots against Albania, but they demonstrated across that tournament a real ability to lock teams out and keep CS, playing fairly conservatively across the group stages.
So why Cucurella?
Spain lack the set-piece potency of some other teams in this tournament
(cough cough England), and therefore their centre backs drop down the list somewhat. Spain’s fullbacks were crucial to their attack at the Euros in 2024, with Carvajal scoring in their first group stage game and Cucurella providing an assist in the final. Cucurella has demonstrated his attacking prowess across multiple Premier League seasons and various international tournaments, so he looks like a good pick considering Spain’s attacking approach. Furthermore,
all of Spain’s starting defenders are already over 5% owned,
meaning that it’s unlikely that any of them will qualify for the scouting bonus, considering their immense popularity.
All things considered, Cucurella is a great option for Spain’s first two fixtures of the WC, offering solid CS potential, decent attacking potential, and secure minutes in what is likely to be a very popular backline in WC fantasy!
Further Read:
2026 World Cup Fantasy Beginner’s Guide: Rules, Tips, and More
Upamecano $5.3m – France
France conceded just 3 goals at Euro 2024
, conceding 1 in the group stage and 2 in their semi-final defeat to Spain. Furthermore, in qualifying,
France conceded just 4 goals across their 6 matches
, highlighting their continued defensive solidity. Les Bleus play Senegal and Iraq in their first two matches of the group stage –
they are ranked 7th for predicted GA
(interactive predicted G and GA are available
here
).
Upamecano is a nailed starter for France going into this tournament
, and is one of the less popular options in France’s backline, currently sat at around 5.1% ownership. With France’s fullbacks offering little attacking threat (and the potential for rotation in wide defensive positions), going for Upamecano is a safe bet for the group stage.
Digne, Hernandez, Gusto, and Kounde were all deployed at fullback
for France across their most recent qualifying and friendly matches, while Konate and Saliba were rotated alongside Upamecano in the other CB slot. No doubt upcoming friendlies will offer more clarity, but Upamecano is still the safest bet. The Bayern defender offers some set-piece threat, though he hasn’t scored for France since 2024 and has only bagged one for the German giants this term.
As in FPL, it’s often the obvious players and teams that do well –
France have played in the last two World Cup finals
and won it in 2018, so we should obviously be targeting their assets. While the attackers will rightly get lots of praise and attention going into the WC, there is real value to be found in their backline.

Argentina
are the reigning world champions and will be keen to prove that their performance at the last edition of the competition was no fluke. According to the aforementioned

Raphinha Dias (Brazil) – $8.2m | TSB: 21.5% ownership

Brazil is stocked with a lot of quality players; however, one player who has been the most consistent for Carlo Ancelotti during the World Cup qualifiers is Raphinha. The Barcelona captain made seven goal contributions (5 goals and two assists) against Bolivia, Peru, Venezuela, and Colombia in Group A of the World Cup qualifiers and is favoured to be Brazil’s no. 1 penalty taker ahead of his teammate, Vinicius Jr. ($10m).
My only predominant worry around Raphinha is likely rotations in his minutes if Brazil appears to secure qualification to the knockout stage after Matchday 2. With the inclusion of Neymar Jr. in the World Cup squad, it’s almost possible to presume Raphinha’s minutes might be impacted going forward. However, I have confidence he won’t lose his place, as Rapha featured in the recent preliminary friendly against Panama and is not far away from hitting 40 caps for Brazil.

Best Teams & Players to Target:
Group
E
(Germany, Curacao, Ivory Coast & Ecuador)
One of the easiest groups this year for Germany, as they are expected to finish as the top team here and to advance to the next round with no difficulty. Although
J. Kimmich ($5.5m-DEF) & D. Raum ($4.9m-DEF)
are clear defensive options with possible attacking returns, there is no obvious pick among their midfielders and forwards.
F. Wirtz ($7.5m-MID), L. Sane ($7.4m-MID), J. Musiala ($8.5m-MID), K. Havertz ($7.8m-FWD),
or even
D. Undav ($6.6m-FWD) & N. Woltemade ($7.2m-FWD)
all might start and get big fantasy points but we still don’t know who to consider due to insecurity of their minutes (or even starting the match), although the first four names are more likely to start for Germany.
So, either of them might award you with big fantasy points, especially in their first match against Curacao, one of the weakest sides of the tournament. Ecuador is the next team to focus on in this group, with
W. Pacho ($4.4m-DEF), P. Hincapie ($4.7m-DEF) & P. Estupinan ($4.8m-DEF)
in defense, and of course their best striker
E. Valencia ($5.9m-FWD)
,
who is available at such a low price, had 8 goal contributions during the qualifications. Ivory Coast and Curacao are the teams to skip fantasy-wise in this group.

Best Teams & Players to Target:
Group
F
(Netherlands, Japan, Sweden & Tunisia
)
Netherlands is expected to have the best performance in this group, with options such as
B. Verbruggen ($4.7m-GK)
in the goal and defenders like
V. van Dijk ($5.5m-DEF) & D. Dumfries ($5.7m-DEF)
,
who are capable of bringing some attacking returns as well as clean sheet points for their owners. In Attack,
C. Gakpo ($7.7m-MID) & M. Depay ($7.4m-FOR)
both look promising with 8G+A & 12G+A respectively in the qualification matches, which are fantastic numbers among all the European players.
Japan is also doing great recently and will probably finish the group stage above Sweden & Tunisia.
Z. Suzuki ($4.3m-GK) & K. Itakura ($4.5m-DEF)
seem to be the most reliable defensive options, while
T. Kubo ($7.0m-MID), J. Ito ($5.0m-MID) & A. Ueda ($7.0m-FWD)
all had at least 10 goal contributions during the qualifying rounds.
V. Gyokeres ($7.8m-FWD)
is obviously the best pick from Sweden, considering his fantastic recent form in the national team, while I wouldn’t suggest any player for your fantasy team from Tunisia.

Best Teams & Players to Target:
Group
G
(Belgium, Egypt, Iran & New Zealand)
Belgium is the favorite to win the group and there are many defensive options among their players, like
T. Courtois ($4.9m-GK), A. Theate ($4.5m-DEF) & Z. Debast ($4.3m-DEF)
,
while
M. De Cuyper ($4.7m-DEF)
can also provide attacking returns like his 2G+2A during the qualification matches, if he starts the match for the team. There are also many midfielders and forwards with good records of goal contributions, but
K. De Bruyne ($7.5m-MID) & J. Doku ($7.5m-MID)
are preferred among them.
M. Salah ($10.0m-MID)
is the clear pick from Egypt despite his high price, as he had a great performance as their talisman during the qualifications with 9G+3A, and
M. El Shenawy ($3.6m-GK) & M. Rabia ($3.6m-DEF)
are trustworthy and cheap among defensive options.
M. Taremi ($6.1m-FWD)
has the same talisman role for Iran as the Asian player with the greatest number of goal contributions (10G+7A) in the qualification round, and
A. Beiranvand ($4.2m-GK) & S. Khalilzadeh ($3.8m-DEF)
as best defensive picks. New Zealand is expected not to finish above any other team in this group, but
C. Wood ($6.5m-FWD)
is surely their best player if you want to back them.

Group
H
(Spain, Cape Verde, Saudi Arabia & Uruguay)
Spain, one of the favorites of the tournament, has one of the easiest groups on paper, as they are expected to advance to the next round without any difficulties.
U. Simon ($5.0m-GK) & M. Cucurella ($5.1m-DEF)
seem to be the safest options in defense based on the record they have in the qualifications.
L. Yamal ($10.0m-MID)
is the best attacking option following his fantastic season in Barcelona with 24G+17A in all competitions. The only concern about him is whether he is fit to play for Spain in their first match, and
D. Olmo ($7.7m-MID)
is the other great pick, especially when Yamal is absent.
M. Oyarzabal ($5.0m-FWD)
is also always in form for Spain, as his 6 goals & 3 assists during the qualification matches prove it.
Uruguay is expected to finish the group stage as the second team, and
S. Rochet ($4.1m-GK) & M. Olivera ($4.3m-DEF)
both seem to be reasonable defensive picks, while selecting the correct attacking player is not easy among
F. Valverde ($7.5m-MID), G. de Arrascaeta ($6.5m-MID) & D. Nunez ($7.5m-FWD)
. The latter had the best attacking record in the qualification round with 5 goals and 2 assists, but he is not guaranteed to start for the team. Saudi Arabia and Cape Verde are two weaker sides in this group with no interesting fantasy options among their players.

In this blog, let’s take a look at the best teams and players to target from FIFA World Cup Groups I-L for our World Cup fantasy teams.
This is part three of our group-wise previews (Link to the other two below):

Best Teams & Players to Target:
Group
I
(France, Senegal, Iraq & Norway):
France, the champion of 2018 and runner-up of 2022, should probably play in the toughest group of the tournament, while they are again one of the favorites this year and surely the most powerful team in the group.
M. Maignan ($5.0m-GK)
is a safe pick in goal, while one of
W. Saliba ($5.3m-DEF), D. Upamecano ($5.3m-DEF) & I. Konate ($4.9m-DEF)
should be benched so that the other two can play as center-back.
J. Kounde ($5.4m-DEF)
can also offer attacking points as a defender, just a similar and maybe safer pick compared to
T. Hernandez ($4.0m-DEF)
. There are also many interesting attacking picks here:
K. Mbappe ($10.5m-FWD)
, the Golden Boot winner of 2022 and their talisman with 47G+A for Real Madrid this season,
O. Dembele ($10.0m-MID)
the PSG superstar,
M. Olise ($9.5m-MID)
with a fantastic record of 22G+26A in Bayern Munchen this season &
R. Cherki ($7.8m-MID)
the young rising star of Manchester City.
Norway and Senegal share the chance to finish as the second team in this group, while Norway counts on
E. Haaland ($10.5m-FWD)
,
as one of the best strikers in the world with the unbelievable record of 16G+3A during the qualification round, Senegal’s talisman is
S. Mane ($7.6m-MID)
. Iraq seems to have such an unlucky draw to be in this group and have the lowest chance to advance to the next round, so there is no interesting fantasy pick among them.

Best Teams & Players to Target:
Group
J
Group J (Argentina, Algeria, Austria & Jordan):
The 2022 champion, Argentina, has relatively easy fixtures in the group stage, and they need to be in form if they wish to defend their crown this year.
E. Martinez ($5.0m-GK) & C. Romero ($4.9m-DEF)
are the safest defensive picks in terms of starting the match, while
N. Otamendi ($4.4m-DEF) & N. Molina ($4.4m-DEF)
may seem a bit risky.
L. Messi ($10.0m-FWD)
is surely the best attacker among them, considering his great form recently and the best record in goal contributions among all South American players during the qualifications.
L. Martinez ($8.8m-FWD) & J. Alvarez ($8.6m-FWD)
are also in form and are good options if we know which one will start, with the second name having mostly been chosen over the other one.
M. Sabitzer ($6.8m-MID) & M. Arnautovic ($6.0m-FWD)
are good fantasy picks from Austria in attack, while
M. Amoura ($6.2m-FWD)
is the best Algerian player with the highest number of goal contributions (10G+4A) among all African players in the qualifying round. Jordan has the lowest odds in this group, so there is no player to suggest for your fantasy team.

Best Teams & Players to Target:
Group
K
Group K (Portugal, DR Congo, Uzbekistan & Colombia):
Portugal is going to play in one of the easiest groups this year, and there are a bunch of players to select from.
D. Costa ($4.9m-GK) & R. Dias ($5.0m-DEF)
are safe options among defensive players, while
J. Cancelo ($5.3m-DEF) & N. Mendes ($5.8m-DEF)
can add attacking returns to the possible clean sheet points.
B. Fernandes ($8.5m-MID)
, the Premier League player of the season, is the safest attacking option with too much potential to return as his current form is fantastic.
C. Ronaldo ($10.0m-FWD) & J. Felix ($6.5m-FWD)
both seem attractive considering the form they had this season together and the thirst they feel to win this year, while the first one is still a better pick.
Colombia can also do a great job in this group and easily finish as the second team with
C. Vargas ($4.3m-GK), D. Munoz ($4.6m-DEF) & D. Sanchez ($4.3m-DEF)
as reliable options in defense.
L. Diaz ($8.1m-MID) & J. Rodriguez ($6.5m-MID)
each had 10 goal contributions during the qualification round, which makes them clear attacking options from their team. Uzbekistan and DR Congo don’t expect to do well in this group, and there is no fantasy suggestion among their players.

Best Teams & Players to Target:
Group
L
Group L (England, Croatia, Ghana & Panama):
England is one of the favorites of the tournament, and they are looking forward to finally winning a trophy this year.
J. Pickford ($4.8m-GK), E. Konsa ($4.8m-DEF), M. Guehi ($5.1m-DEF) & R. James ($5.2m-DEF)
are expected to start the match and are considered their best defensive options.
H. Kane ($10.5m- FWD)
, with an unbelievable season with Bayern Munich (58G+7A all competitions), is clearly their attacking pick, while it would be difficult to select among
J. Bellingham ($8.3m-MID), B. Saka ($9.5m-MID) & D. Rice ($7.0m-MID)
as the second option.
Croatia is predicted to finish behind England with
D. Livakovic ($4.5m-GK), J. Gvardiol ($5.0m-DEF) & J. Stanisic ($4.3m-DEF)
in the defense line and
I. Perisic ($5.4m-FWD) & A. Kramaric ($6.2m-FWD)
as their attacking options with good form and history.
A. Semenyo ($7.2m-FWD) & J. Ayew ($5.3m-FWD)
are also interesting picks from Ghana if you wish to select any of their players, while there is no interesting option from Panama to target.

Best 2026 FIFA World Cup Fantasy MD1 Drafts
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is fast approaching us and there is excitement amongst football fans and fantasy football lovers who can’t wait for the tournament to get started. The official 2026 FIFA World Cup Fantasy Football game has also been launched, which means it’s time to get started in setting up our teams ahead of the matchday 1 deadline, which is the kick-off of the first game between Mexico and South Africa on the 11th of June. In this blog, let’s look at some of the best MD1 World Cup Fantasy drafts with pros and cons for each draft.
Link to all our 2026 World Cup Fant
asy blogs
in one place, including position-wise players to target, Group previews, Differentials, Captaincy, Chip strategy, Budget Enablers, Tips, and more. Completely free to access as well!
Best World Cup Fantasy MD1 drafts with Pros/Cons
Matchday 1 is the longest matchday this time, with
24 games
being held over eight days from 11th June to 18th June. All the games are listed below for your reference
A quick overview of the rules:
There are eight matchdays, and the initial focus is on the three matchdays during the group stages, with managers getting unlimited transfers between MD3 & MD4 (Round of 32)
Five boosters make it exciting, as you’ll have to use them in the 8 Matchdays
You can manually substitute players between any match, ofcourse you can only sub in a player who hasn’t played yet. The same applies to captaincy, too.
Having multiple captaincy options is essential as you change your captain if they blank or do not haul big.
Popular chip/boost strategies in the group stage include: Wildcard in MD2 or Wildcard in MD3, 12th man in MD2/MD3.

World Cup Fantasy MD1 Draft 1
This draft is tripled up on Spain, who have the best predicted goals both for and against in MD1 as they face Cabo Verde.
There’s a nice balance of differentials under <5% ownership to target the scouting bonus, including the likes of Enner Valencia, James Rodriguez, Çalhanoglu, and De Cuyper.
Haaland is in this draft with Norway facing Iraq in MD1, giving him a favourable matchup. If you’re not bullish on Haaland beyond MD1, you can use the 12th man booster and have him for MD1 alone, too.
Ryerson is another differential from Norway who has been in very good attacking form of late.

World Cup Fantasy MD1 Draft 2
This draft goes cheap with the Goalkeepers with St Clair & Rochet in goal, both of them qualify for scouting bonus too.
Lamine Yamal and Oyarzabal are the two Spanish attackers in this draft to maximize Spain’s matchup against Cabo Varde in MD1.
Oyarzabal in 2026 World Cup Qualifiers: 442 minutes, 6 Goals, 3 Assists, 1.83 Goal Contributions/90
Top Players for Goal Contributions from Europe in World Cup Qualifying
Two Brazil players will setu you up well for MD2 when they face Haiti, Both Wesley & Bruno Guimares qualify for table bonus too.
Xhaka and Coufal are the outfield players under 5% ownership qualifying for table bonus.

World Cup Fantasy MD1 Draft 3
Ofcourse we cannot go without having some options from the defending champions, Argentina
Embolo is a strong differential not seen in many drafts playing for a strong Swiss side who’ll be facing Qatar. Kane & Messi complete the forward line and this combination of Embolo, Kane & Messi is far from the popular template of Oyarzabal, Mbappe & Haaland
Olise covers France attack for MD1 and McTominay is the differential in midfield.
This draft has three of the most-in form midfielders heading into the World Cup in the form of Raphinha, Olise and Yamal who have all had phenomenal seasons for their clubs.

World Cup Fantasy MD1 Draft 4
All of the five defenders (Cucurella, De Cuyper, Kimmich, Munoz & Nuno Mendes) in this draft are attacking-minded, and the haul potential is high with them.
Going slightly heavy in defence means we have to make up for it in midfield with Williams in place of Yamal. Sabitzer and Rodriguez are the differential midfielders in this draft.

World Cup 2026: Most In-Form Players in Each Position
World Cup 2026 is just around the corner, and here we take a look at the most in-form players based on their performances for their club during the season, who are expected to continue their form in the upcoming competition.
Link to all our 2026 World Cup Fant
asy blogs
in one place, including position-wise players to target, Group previews, Differentials, Captaincy, Chip strategy, Budget Enablers, Tips, and more. Completely free to access as well!
(note: G=Goal, A=Assist)
World Cup 2026 Most In-Form Players – Forwards:
Note:
The tables are interactive, and you can click on the column headers to sort them as you wish.
Harry Kane ($10.5m-England):
European Golden Shoe winner of the season and one of the best forwards in the game, who just spent his best-ever season with Bayern Munich, has the exceptional record of 68 goal contributions (61G + 7A) in all competitions.
Erling Haaland ($10.5m-Norway):
Premier League Golden Boot winner contributed in 47 goals for Manchester City in all competitions, including 38 goals and 9 assists.
Lionel Messi ($10.0m-Argentina):
The Golden Ball winner of the previous World Cup tournament is running the show at the age of 38, with 13 goals & 7 assists in his 16 appearances for Inter Miami this season so far.
Kylian Mbappe ($10.5m-France):
Although some believe he wasn’t at his peak this season in Real Madrid, he still managed to be the top goal scorer of the Champions League and LaLiga with a total of 42 goals and 6 assists in all competitions.
Julian Alvarez ($8.6m-Argentina):
The best Atletico Madrid player of the season, scored 20 goals and provided 9 assists in all competitions for the club.
Lautaro Martinez ($8.8m-Argentina):
The top goal scorer of Serie A had 28 goal contributions this season (22G + 6A) in all the matches he played for Inter Milan.
Joao Felix ($6.5m-Portugal) / Cristiano Ronaldo ($10.0m-Portugal):
Portuguese forwards played well alongside each other at Al-Nassr this season, which led them to have 33 (20G + 13A) & 30 (28G + 2A) goal contributions respectively.
Luis Suarez ($5.7m-Colombia):
Top scorer of Liga Portugal with 40 goal contributions (33G + 7A) for Sporting across all competitions this season.
World Cup 2026 Most In-Form Players –
Midfielders
Bruno Fernandes ($8.5m-Portugal):
The best player of the Premier League and the assist record breaker with 31 goal contributions (9G + 22A) in all competitions for Manchester United.
Micheal Olise ($9.5m-France):
The French young star with a phenomenal season at Bayern Munich scored 22 goals and provided 26 assists for his teammates.
Lamine Yamal ($10.0m-Spain):
The Best player of LaLiga in the previous season was involved in 41 goals for Barcelona (24G + 14A).
Ousmane Dembele ($10.0m-France):
The League 1 Player of the Season and the Champions League winner contributed in 31 goals for PSG this season (20G + 11A).
Luis Diaz ($8.1m-Colombia):
Alongside Kane and Olise, he also spent a wonderful year during his first season at Bayern Munich, including 26 goals & 19 assists.
Raphinha ($8.2m-Brazil):
Despite missing a part of the season due to injury, he still could score 21 goals and provide 7 assists in the 33 games he played for Barcelona.
Vinicius Jr ($10.0m-Brazil):
Scoring 22 goals and providing 10 assists in all competitions for Real Madrid proves he is doing great, even if it’s not his best season.
Rayan Cherki ($8.0m-France):
The French attacking midfielder, who recently won Premier League Young Player of the Season, had 25 goal contributions (10G + 15A) in all competitions for Manchester City.
World Cup 2026 Most In-Form Players –
Defenders
Gabriel ($5.5m-Brazil):
Arguably the main reason Arsenal could win the Premier League after 22 years, and their best player with such a great performance in defense was involved in 3G+4A in the league.
Ashraf Hakimi ($6.0m- Morocco):
Although he missed some periods of the season due to injury, he still managed to have 11 goal contributions (3G + 8A) for PSG.
Nuno Mendes ($5.8m-Portugal):
The best left full-back in the world had a record of 6 goals and 7 assists for PSG across all competitions this season.
Joshua Kimmich ($5.5m-Germany):
One of the best players of Bayern Munich in their wonderful run this season, had 14 goal contributions, including 3G+12A in all competitions.
Nico O’Reilly (4.7m-England):
What a breakthrough for the 21-year-old Man City young star this season, with the record of 9 goals & 6 assists in all competitions for the club.

World Cup Fantasy 2026: Comprehensive Booster Strategy Guide
Let’s check the
Boosters in the FIFA World Cup 2026 Fantasy official game
and see how each one can be used according to your strategy. While managers’ strategies might change during the tournament based on what happens, each Booster can still be used in a safe or risky way, and the latter makes you a manager with a “Differential” strategy in terms of Boosters.
Link to all our 2026 World Cup Fant
asy blogs
in one place, including position-wise players to target, Group previews, Differentials, Captaincy, Chip strategy, Budget Enablers, Tips, and more. Completely free to access as well!
World Cup Fantasy Booster Strategy Guide
There are
five boosters in World Cup fantasy
. Let’s look at the popular and differential windows to use each one of them.
World Cup Fantasy Booster: WILDCARD
What does it do? You have unlimited transfers within a specific round. It cannot be used for the first match of the group stage or round of 32, because you already have unlimited transfers for these two rounds.
Popular: Round 3
In general, it makes sense to use Wildcard in round 3 as a “Free Hit” when most of the teams will probably have rotations in their squads. Especially in the new format of the competition with 48 teams, where bigger names face weaker sides in the group stage, most of the popular fantasy picks in rounds 1 & 2 will be benched because they are secured to advance to the next round. This is a reasonable opportunity to use your Wildcard to fundamentally change your team to bring in players who have something to fight for.
Further Read
:
World Cup 2026: Predicted Goals (For & Against) – Interactive Table
Differential: Round of 16 or Quarter-finals
International competitions like the World Cup 2022, Euro 2020 & Euro 2024 have shown us that surprising results happen during the knockout stage. Croatia over Brazil, Morocco over Spain & Portugal, Switzerland over France & Italy, Czechia over Netherlands, all had the lowest odds of happening, and they took place while nobody really expected. Saving your Wildcard for the knockout stage will provide you with the chance to select your players without fear because you know that if anything exceptional happens, you can still save your team with this booster.
World Cup Fantasy Booster: MAXIMUM CAPTAIN
What does it do? You get double points from whichever player scores the most points from your starting eleven. This player will automatically be assigned the captaincy.
Popular: Final
Because the captain can be changed unlimitedly during a round (as long as the new selected captain is yet to play and your previous captain has completed his game), it makes sense to use this booster in a round with the minimum number of matches, which is the final. Especially in the first five rounds, there are a lot of opportunities to change the captain (between 8 and 10 each round) and to select a new captain to finally get satisfied with the points.
The only problem here is that the ceiling of the players’ fantasy points in the final of such a competition is not that high in general, OR maybe selecting the captain might not be that difficult! In the last two finals, when there were a lot of goals, going for either Messi/Mbappe in 2022 or Griezmann/Mbappe/Pogba in 2018 worked well with no such big differences among them. On the other hand, in 2014, 2010, and 2006, we had no player with more than 7 fantasy points. My conclusion is that, whether the final is predicted to have many goals or not, using this booster might not be that helpful. In the first scenario (a lot of goals), we can probably recognize the best players for captaincy, and in the second scenario (low number of goals), the most fantasy points of any player are not that much. Either way, maybe using the 12th Man in the final is not as optimal as it seems at first!
Differential: Semi-final
International competitions like the World Cup 2022, Euro 2020 & Euro 2024 have shown us that surprising results can happen during the knockout stage. Croatia over Brazil, Morocco over Spain & Portugal, Switzerland over France & Italy, Czechia over Netherlands, all had the lowest odds of happening, while they took place when nobody expected them. Saving your Wildcard for the knockout stage will provide you with the chance to select your players without fear because you know that if anything exceptional happens, you can still save your team with this booster.
Further Read
:
World Cup 2026: Most In-Form Players in Each Position
– Interactive Table
12th MAN
What does it do? You select one additional player to score points for your team in a round as he cannot be substituted, captained, or transferred. He can be any player as long as that player is not already part of your squad. Budget or team restrictions do not count for him.
Popular: Round 1 or Round 2
Group stage matches are generally more favorable for big fantasy points returns, as there are more “strong team vs weak team” confrontations, which lead to one-sided matches (more goals and more clean sheets). Considering the budget restriction in these 3 rounds, having a team that includes all the big names to achieve maximum points seems difficult, and that’s the reason this booster is preferred for one of the group stage rounds. As there are many boosters and you don’t waste any, and considering that most of them are not worth using in the first 2 matches (assume using Wildcard in round 3), this is absolutely a logical decision to use your 12th Man in round 1 or round 2.
Differential: Round 3
There is a strategy for those who want to avoid using their Wildcard in the group stage by keeping it for the knockout stage:
Since round 3 will probably have the most squad rotations, it’s best to have the maximum number of free transfers for this round. To achieve that, you use only one free transfer for round 2, so you carry over the other one to round 3. Then, in round 3, you will have three free transfers, and assuming that you use your 12th Man booster in this round, you can bring in four new players (three with transfers + one with 12th Man), which is the highest number you can have without getting a hit. In this case, saving the 12th Man booster for round 3 will be the differential way to play it.
Further Read:
Best 2026 FIFA World Cup Fantasy MD1 Drafts
QUALIFICATION BOOSTER
What does it do? You grant +2 points to any player in your starting eleven who progresses to the next round of the knockout stage or wins the final (from round of 32 onwards).
Popular: Round of 32
Round of 16 seems the optimal time to use this booster, as it’s the knockout stage where we probably see the most amount of “strong team vs weak team” matches. So it’s clearly the right moment to use this booster to achieve the highest possible points.
Differential: Round of 16
Although it does not seem optimal to use this booster after the round of 32, the next round, which is the round of 16, is the second and maybe the final chance to activate this booster. There will probably be enough favorable matches where one team has much higher odds of winning and advancing in the competition.

MYSTERY BOOSTER (Clean Sheet Shield)
Note: This booster hasn’t been officially announced yet, and it will be available from the round of 32 onwards, though it might probably be “Clean Sheet Shield”.
What does it do? Any goalkeeper, defender, or midfielder will only lose their clean sheet after conceding 2 goals.
Popular: Round of 16

Best Goalkeepers to Target in FIFA 2026 World Cup Fantasy
A team’s structure loses its foundation without the right guard protecting its defense. In making your final World Cup fantasy selections, one position that cannot be overlooked is the goalkeeping position. In this article, we’ll list the best goalkeepers to target for the group stage of the FIFA 2026 World Cup.

Thibaut Courtois (Belgium) $4.9m | TSB: 12.1% ownership
Group Stage Fixtures
: Egypt, IR Iran, New Zealand
The 34-year-old has 108 caps and 55 clean sheets to his name for the Belgian Red Devils. Thibaut Courtois brings in a wealth of experience standing in goal for Belgium as he features in his fourth World Cup tournament since 2014. The Real Madrid shot-stopper recently kept a clean sheet in the 2-0 victory against Croatia and will be set to replicate a string of clean sheets in Group D.
When to Target
: Despite Courtois’ experience, I only see him featuring in the first two matches against Egypt and Iran. If the Belgians finish with four to six points after Matchday 2, I believe his deputy,
Senne Lammens
($4.6m), will get some minutes in the last group game against New Zealand.

ó
n (Spain) $5m | TSB: 6.7% ownership

Cape Verde, Saudi Arabia, Uruguay

. Think of it like the

Unai

was solid in goal for Spain during the Euro 2024 contest, keeping 10 clean sheets in 12 appearances. Simon also started all six matches for La Roja during the World Cup Qualifiers, securing five clean sheets and conceding only two. Spain are clear favorites to go all the way to the final, and Unai Simón looks like a must-have fantasy pick in Group H.
When to Target
: Spain has three excellent keepers, Raya, Simon and Barcelona’s
Joan Garcia
($4m). However, pending any potential injuries or team rotation, if you still own Raya before Matchday 1, now is the time to swap to Unai Simón, who will likely start all group stage matches.

Bart Verbruggen (Netherlands) $4.7m | TSB: 3.6% ownership
Group Stage Fixtures:
Japan, Sweden, Tunisia
The Netherlands are group favorites to top Group F this World Cup, so it’s a bit surprising seeing Brighton’s goalkeeper,
Bart Verbruggen
, with less than 5% ownership among fantasy managers. This means he currently qualifies for the scouting bonus system and was phenomenal for the Oranje team during the World Cup qualifiers, keeping three clean sheets in six appearances.
When to Target
: Although the Dutch team are Group F favourites. Their recent shock 1-0 defeat against Algeria shows that they are not entirely invincible. Nonetheless, if you are trying to save budget from the $5m keepers and searching for less-owned assets for your team, then Verbruggen is one to consider.

FIFA World Cup 2026 – Penalty and Set Piece Takers of all 48 Teams
Jordan Pickford (England) $4.8m | TSB: 14.6% ownership
Group Stage Fixtures
: Croatia, Ghana, Panama
Jordan Pickford, who is playing in his third World Cup for the Three Lions, now has 82 caps for his country after making his debut in November 2017. The 32-year-old started seven out of eight matches during the World Cup qualifiers, keeping seven clean sheets and not conceding a single goal.
When to Target
: England are no doubt the best defensive team in the tournament, and Pickford is a must-have pick to have in your fantasy team from Group L.
Further Read:
World Cup 2026: Most In-Form Players in Each Position
Emiliano Martinez (Argentina) $5m | TSB: 22.2% ownership
Group Stage Fixtures:
Algeria, Austria, Jordan
Argentina, the defending champions, are back on the World Cup stage, and returning is 2023 and 2024 Yashin Trophy winner Emiliano Martinez. The 33-year-old has kept six clean sheets in his last 10 matches for Argentina, accumulating to a total of 40 clean sheets in 59 caps for his nation.
When to Target
: No need to overthink it; Martinez is a must-have keeper who rotates nicely with a cheaper goalie when Argentina play among the latter fixtures in Group J.
Further Read:
Best 2026 FIFA World Cup Fantasy MD1 Drafts
Manuel Neuer (Germany) $5m | TSB: 13.2% ownership
Group Stage Fixtures:
Curaçao, Côte d’Ivoire, Ecuador
Despite struggling with injuries and being missing from the German squad since September 2024, the 40-year-old’s league performances with Bayern Munich have earned him a call-up for his fifth World Cup tournament with the DFB team.
Neuer kept six clean sheets in 22 league games for Bayern Munich; however, where the veteran shines most is from his high claim and number of saves made. This makes him a huge addition for Germany when they take on 83rd place, Curacao, who will be making their World Cup debut.
When to Target
: Julian Nagelsmann has passed Neuer to be fit for the first game; however, it seems he could be rested in future games whenever there is a sign of an injury risk. With this in mind, if you’re considering Neuer, you should consider having
Oliver Baumann
($4.3m) as his replacement or backup goalie, who started the majority of the World Cup qualifiers.

Darwin Núñez: What Could He Mean For FPL Managers?
Darwin Nunez
had a very impressive 21/22 season both domestically, in the Portugese League (Primeira Liga), and in the Champions League with Benfica. Following his huge move to Liverpool, which cost £85m after fees, this article will analyse his statistics and consider whether he should be as popular a pick as the likes of Haaland, Salah, Son, or KDB. I will also look at how other signings have adapted to the Premier League from the Primeira Liga to give us more of an idea of what we can expect from Darwin Nunez – and his FPL potential.

Núñez’s figures dramatically improved from 20/21 to 21/22. Last season, he had an expected goal involvement of 1.05 per 90 minutes, while actually achieving a goal involvement of 1.36 per 90. He also earned the top scorer award, beating his closest rival by 6 goals.
He has also impressed in international competitions. In the 2020/21 Europa League campaign, he scored 5 goals in 7 appearances. Most recently, in the 2021/22 Champions League, he scored 6 goals in 10 games, including 2 against Liverpool in the quarter-finals.
We can also see that Núñez really over-performed his expected goal stats, showing great accuracy and finishing.
It is also worth noting that with Benfica he also had the advantage of being on penalties, scoring 4 in total. This won’t be the case at Liverpool, but 22 non-penalty goals is still impressive.
The article below outlines Núñez’s playing style. To summarise, Núñez plays centrally, but can play on the left wing.
https://www.espn.co.uk/football/blog-soccer-transfers/story/4684619/darwin-nunez-to-liverpool-the-stats-behind-his-amazing-breakout-year-that-led-to-anfield-transfer
He also scored 5 headed goals last season, and, being 6″2, offers a great goal threat to crosses from the wingbacks.

What Can We Expect from Núñez in His First Season?
Although it can be hard to predict how a player will adjust to playing in a new league, we can look at how other players have adapted to the Premier League from Portugal.
Lazar Marković
Numbers for Lazar Marković’s expected statistics are not entirely available, but he moved to Liverpool from Benfica in 2014 under Brendan Rodgers. In all competitions, he scored 3 goals in 34 appearances for Liverpool, and had several loan spells before finally leaving to Fulham on a free transfer. In his only season with Benfica, he scored 5 goals in 26 appearances and won a domestic treble.
[ Marković transferred to Liverpool from Benfica in 2014 for a total cost of around £22.5m ]

Last season, he had 0 goals (xG = 2.2) and 0 assists (xA = 0.7) from playing only 160 minutes. He has been unfortunate with playing time, and so hasn’t really found his feet in the Premier League yet, but is still only 19 years old.

Bruno Fernandes became a solid FPL asset when he made the move from Sporting Lisbon to United. In the 2018/19 season with Lisbon, he was 2nd among the top goalscorers and for the most assists. He continued this form in the first half of the next season, before moving to United and making an instant impact.
In his first full season with United, he created the most chances (including big chances), had the joint-2nd highest number of assists, and was 3rd in the Golden Boot race. Expected data is not available for his time in Portugal, but he scored 18 goals from an xG of 16.1 and bagged 12 assists from an xA of 9.7 in 2020/21.
With United’s dip in form, he had a poorer 2021/22 season. Despite this, he was still 2nd for chances created and in the top 10 for big chances created.
It’s clear that Fernandes’ stats did translate to the Premier League, so this does show that it is possible for Núñez to have a strong first season.

Premier League Players Who’ll be Missing the 2022 World Cup
The 2022 world cup will be held in the middle of the 2022/23 Premier League season & in this blog, we’ll see the list of 30+ premier league players who’ll not be going to the world cup and instead getting a nice break during the middle of the season. We’ll also cover the impacts on each of the 20 PL clubs and the best 11 from the premier league that will not go to the World Cup.
Note:
It’s only the players who’s national teams have not qualified for the World cup and the list doesn’t involve players that might not be potentially picked in the WC squad.
Time Line of the 2022 FIFA World Cup
The final 32 teams who’ll be playing at the 2022 FIFA World Cup
November 13th: Premier League goes on break
November 21st: World Cup starts
December 2nd:
End of the group stages
December 3rd:
Round of 16 begins
December 6th:
Round of 16 ends
December 9th&10th:
Four Quarterfinal games
December 13th & 14th:
Semifinals
December 17th: 3rd place game
December 18th: World Cup finals
December 20th/21st: Carabao cup fourth round(Return of English club football)
December 26: Premier League resumes
Premier League players not going to the World cup are now expected to have a 41-day break during the middle of the season.
Players who’ll miss the 2022 Fifa World Cup
Mo Salah, Egypt, Liverpool
2021/22 Premier League golden boot winner will not be going to the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar after Egypt failed to beat Senegal in the playoffs. Salah’s side lost to Senegal on penalties after both the sides for level after two legs. Egypt also lost the Africa cup of nations on penalties to Senegal in Feb which made it a double break for Salah and Egypt. Although Salah not going to the World cup is welcome news for Klopp and Liverpool with the attacker now expected to get a good break during the middle of the season.
Mohamed Elneny, Egypt, Arsenal
Mo Salah’s national teammate Mohamed Elneny(Arsenal) who signed a contract extension with Arsenal will also miss out on a WC spot.
Erling Haaland, Norway, Man City
One of the most talented youngsters in World football right now, Haaland will not be playing in his first world cup after Norway failed to qualify for the tournament. Haaland has been in sensational form for Man City and the break will be a welcome sign for Pep and Man City as his star player will be getting a much-needed break. This could also mean Pep would play him a lot more until the world cup break which is what all of the 80% plus FPL managers who own him in FPL would want.
Martin Ødegaard Norway, Arsenal
Haaland’s national teammate and Arsenal captain, Martin Ødegaard(Arsenal) will also miss out on his first FIFA World Cup this year. The other players from norway who’ll not be at the WC include Ajer(Brentford), M. Elyounoussi(Norway), K Klaesson(Leeds) and Hjelde(Leeds)

Andy Robertson, Scotland, Liverpool
Robertson has been an ever-present figure for Liverpool over the last few years and is one of the most important players for Liverpool under Klopp. Robertson’s Scotland lost to Ukraine in the playoffs and missed the chance to go to the world cup which gives him a nice little break during the season. Klopp has another good news with Robertson’s deputy
Kostas Tsimikas(Greece) also not going to the WC after Greece failed to qualify
.

Riyad Mahrez, Algeria, Man City
Man City star Riyad Mahrez will not be going to the World cup after Algeria lost to Cameroon on away goals in the World Cup playoffs. Mahrez had played only one game before in the world cup which was in 2014 against Belgium. He has however scored 26 goals and assisted 34 goals for Algeria in his international career. It’s huge welcome news for Pep as he’ll have two of his biggest attackers not going to the World Cup and instead will be getting a good break.
Saïd Benrahma(West Ham)
Saïd Benrahma(West Ham) national teammate of Mahrez will also miss out on an opportunity to play in his first world cup and will have to wait until the 2026 WC minimum.
Luis Diaz, Colombia, Liverpool
Luis Diaz proved to be the best January signing last season and played a crucial role in Liverpool competing for four trophies till the very end of the season. He has also started the 2022/23 season brightly but Unfortunately, Diaz failed to qualify for the World Cup with Columbia who’ll miss out on the biggest competition of world football after featuring in the 2014 and 2018 editions. Columbia’s loss is Liverpool’s gain with Diaz now getting a break when the World Cup is played later in 2022.

Wilfred Zaha, Ivory Coast, Crystal Palace
Zaha who once played for England U21 and England national team switched allegiance to Ivory Coast in 2017. Zaha and Ivory Coast however did not play in the 2018 World cup and also failed to qualify for the 2022 edition. Ivory coast however played all the three previous editions in 2006,2010 and 2018. Zaha is the talisman for Crystal Palace and his absence from the World cup will be welcome news for Palace and Vieira.
Other Ivory Coast national players(PL only) who’ll not go to the 2022 FIFA World Cup
Sergie Aurier(Nottingham Forest) and Amad Diallo(Man United) will also miss out on going to his first World cup.
Iheanacho, Nigeria, Leicester City
Foxes forward Iheancho who played his first world cup in 2018 will miss out on this edition of the World cup after Nigeria failed to qualify for the first time since 2006. Iheanacho played 148 minutes in the 2018 WC but did not score a goal and it looks like his wait for a WC goal is going to take a while with the 2026 edition being his next chance. Leicester City will be huge beneficiaries with Iheanacho along with Nididi, and Lookman all missing out on a WC trip. The other foxes forwards

We already have the full timeline of Champions League, Europa League, Conference League and Carabao cup for the 2022/23 season with FA Cup fixture dates to be announced, but there shouldn’t be big changes in the FA Cup main rounds as it generally starts in the first weekend of the new yeat by when the Wolrd cup will be over.

Can I change my FPL Team name?
Yes, you can change your FPL Team name any number of times.
How to change my FPL Team Name?
You can change your FPL Team name by clicking on the “My Teams Kit” on the points page. You’ll have the option to change your team name, jersey, and more
Can offensive team names be reported?
Offensive team names can be reported by clicking on the report offensive team name option on a particular FPL manager’s points page
How long can my FPL team name be?
FPL Team name can be of a maximum length of 20 characters and not more than that.

Man City fixtures (CAPS home/Small away)
We will start off by looking at the Champions of the premier league. Manchester City, like much of the top six, have a great start to their campaign.
This is what their first 8 GWs look like: West Ham (A), Bournemouth (H), Newcastle (A), Crystal Palace (H), Nottingham Forest (H), Aston Villa (A), Tottenham (H), and Wolves (A). (
Further Read:
Initial Reaction to Top Six PL Teams Fixtures for the 22/23 Season
)
FPL Players to target from Manchester City:
Erling Haaland (11.5 Million)
The new signing for Manchester City arrives with great expectations into England.
Haaland
is a known goalscorer. At Molde, he scored 14 goals in 39 appearances, at RB Salzburg he scored 17 goals in 16 games and then at Borussia Dortmund he scored an incredible 62 goals in 67 appearances
His non-penalty goal contribution per 90 is at a whopping 0.93 per 90, with an npxG of 0.67 and xA of 0.26. Half of his shorts on target result in goals and around 44% of his total shorts are on target. This means Haaland scores with almost every fifth shot that he takes!
He is a proven attacker and at 11.5, he is (at the time of writing) the third-most-expensive player. A strong captaincy shout from GW1 itself, Haaland should be in everyone’s fpl team.
Kevin De Bruyne (12 Million)
The all-time reliable Kevin De Bruyne is priced at 12 million this season, the second-highest at the time of writing. If you plan to have both Haaland and Salah in your team, squeezing in KDB can be a little difficult.
Last season he averaged 0.52 goals per 90, with an xG of 0.25 and xA of 0.37 per 90. He also played 3.47 key passes per 90, while 5.68 shot-creating actions and 0.58 goal-creating actions per 90.
KDB also averaged 4.54 touches in the opponent’s penalty box, showing his attacking instincts.
Kevin De Bruyne has always been a reliable top-six player and this season will be no different. However, can you squeeze him into a team with Haaland and Salah this season?
João Cancelo (7 Million)
Priced at 7 million, Joao Cancelo is a very good option for any team. He can be one of the two premium players alongside Trent Alexander-Arnold in defence.
Unlike most of Manchester City’s players, Cancelo’s position in the team is more or less cemented. The 28-year-old featured in 36 out of 38 games last season, missing one of them due to suspension.
Playing for the best defence in the league, Cancelo offers a lot of promise while attacking. His npxG + xA last season was 0.28 per 90, while averaging 2.04 shots per 90 with 0.6 of them being on target.
He also created 2.90 shots creating action per 90 and 0.44 goal-creating actions per 90. He also played 7.35 passes into the final third per 90.
Honorable mention: Ruben Dias

Liverpool fixtures (CAPS home/Small away)
Time to look at the second team from the top six, Liverpool. The Reds’ first eight fixtures, just like Manchester City’s, look really good. They play these teams in their first eight fixtures: Fulham (A), Crystal Palace (H), Manchester United (A), Bournemouth (H), Newcastle (H), Everton (A), Wolves (H), Chelsea (A).
FPL Players to target from Liverpool:
Mohamed Salah (13 Million)
Considered the best player in the world by some, Salah was the highest point-getter in fpl last season as he got the most goals and the most assists last season. With him signing a new contract with Liverpool, Salah is set to terrorize premier league defence for more seasons to come.
In 35 games last season, Salah scored 23 times and assisted a further 13 times. The Egyptian had an expected non-penalty goal contribution of 0.85 per game. He had 4.18 shots per game with 1.65 of them on target. He also averaged around 10.54 touches in the opponent’s penalty box.
He is also the penalty taker for Liverpool adding an extra bonus.
Salah has to be the first name on everyone’s first team as he offers potential captaincy candidacy every single game that he plays.
Trent Alexander-Arnold (7.5 Million)
At 7.5 million, Trent Alexander-Arnold is a bargain. Alongside Cancelo, Trent should be a mainstay in your fpl team from the first gameweek. Playing for the second-best defence in the league, Trent, like Cancelo, offers a very high ceiling when it comes to attacking points.
In 32 games last season, Trent scored twice and assisted a further 12 goals. He averaged an expected non-penalty goal contribution of 0.42 per 90, while also averaging 1.58 shots per 90. He played 2.77 key passes per 90, 7.58 passes into the final third, and 2.64 passes into the final third per 90.
He also created 4.50 shot-creating actions per 90, while averaging 2.57 touches per 90 in the opponent’s penalty area.
While we are talking about Trent, it is important that we compare his stats to that of Andy Robertson, who is priced at 7 million.
Trent VS Robertson (per 90)
xA: 0.35 vs 0.19
npxG + xA: 0.42 vs 0.24
Shots per 90: 1.58 vs 0.62
Crosses into penalty area: 0.84 vs 0.48
Shot-Creating actions: 4.50 vs 3.51
Touches in opponent’s penalty box: 2.57 vs 3.02
As you can see Trent beats Robertson in most areas, hence making him a safer pick for most teams. However, Robertson would be a good differential pick for you.
Luis Diaz (8 million)
With Sadio Mane leaving Anfield, Luis Diaz is set to become the starting LW in this Liverpool squad. We saw a glimpse of what he can do last season but now given a starting spot the sky is the limit for him.
Last season in thirteen appearances in the premier league, he already scored 4 goals and assisted a further 3.
He averaged an expected non-penalty goal contribution of 0.6 per 90, while averaging 3.57 shots per 90. He also created 4.60 shots creating action per 90.
With Jota being named as a forward for 9 million this season, his appeal has gone down, especially with the arrival of Darwin Nunez, his starts are set to be reduced.
Honourable Mentions: Darwin Nunez, Diogo Jota, Joel Matip, Virgil Van Dijk

2022/23 FPL Season Launch | What’s new in FPL?
There are no changes with respect to points scoring, transfers, hits for the upcoming FPL season.
Chip changes in FPL:
The rules for all the chips are the same as in the previous seasons. You will have the same Free Hit, Bench Boost and Triple Captain which can be used only once per season.
Wildcard
The
Wildcard
chip can be used twice a season. The first wildcard will be available from the start of the season until 12 November, 2022– GW16 deadline.
After GW16, Premier League action will stop and the players will disperse for the World Cup.
Premier League(GW17) will resume on 26th December, 2022
The second wildcard will be available after 26 December, 2022 in readiness for the January transfer window opening and remain available until the end of the season(GW38).
How was the Wildcard rule in 2021/22 FPL season(previous season)?
The rules of the Wildcard chip were same for the previous season– Unlimited transfers and can be used twice in a season. But the second Wildcard can be used by only after December in the previous season. This is changed to GW16 for this season due to
World Cup
.
Unlimited Transfers before between GW16 and GW17 deadline
Unlimited free transfers can also be made between 12 November, 2022 and 26 December,2022 when the Premier League season has been adjusted to accommodate the FIFA World Cup Qatar 2022.
This can be put like an equivalent of a Third Wildcard. To put in simple terms,
First Wildcard- Should be used before GW16
Second Wildcard- Unlimited transfers for GW17 deadline when PL resume after World Cup
Third Wildcard- Can be used anywhere between GW18 and GW38.

Transfer Changes:
In a normal gameweek, you are restricted to 20 transfers only. This does not apply when you have the WC and FH active or during the WC break when you’ll have unlimited transfers.
Two Factor Authentication
FPL has introduced the two-factor authentication for the upcoming season which helps keeps our account safe and is recommended by us. Here is how you can enable two factor authentication for your FPL account.

@OfficialFPL
account

https://t.co/mxZiRMqb8n
& click manage profile
2️⃣Click on Account security
3️⃣Enable two factor authentication by scanning the code or entering the key on a authenticator app✅

How Will Five Substitutions Per Game Impact  2022/23 FPL Season?
2022/23 FPL Pre Season Ultimate Guide
Game week Preview
July 7, 2022
July 12, 2022
Deevyashali Parekh
How Will Five Substitutions Per Game Impact  2022/23 FPL Season?
The Premier League voted to change from 3 substitutions to 5 per team from the upcoming Premier League 22/23 campaign, similar to Project Restart and recent Champions League games. The rule change means that teams in the Premier League will now be able to name 9 substitutes on the bench rather than 7 and make up to 5 substitutions during the game rather than 3. These 5 subs will have to be made in only 3 ‘instances’ of substitutions or at halftime.
Link to all our 2022/23 Pre Season articles
including a transfer tracker of all new signings, all team pre-season friendly updates, team previews, fixture analysis, position-wise analysis, and more. Completely free to access as well!
Initial instinct on looking at the league increasing the number of subs from 3 to 5 made me feel like we might see more rotation and reduced minutes for popular FPL assets.  However, on the evidence of ‘Project Restart’ where the same sub rules were instituted, most FPL assets ended up getting rotated lesser but had a higher chance of being subbed off early.
‘Project Restart’
It’s hard to extrapolate data from a unique, COVID-hit scenario where the season had to be halted and then resumed after a delay. It was an unprecedented event that involved its own unique challenges which are unlikely to be replicated into a normal, full season of the Premier League. Nonetheless, given that that period of time, called ‘Project Restart’ was the only previous evidence of these rules in the Premier League, let’s talk a quick look at how clubs fared during that time frame.
What effect did five subs have on goals scored and conceded?
With the caveat of different fixtures, managers and an idiosyncratic scenario, let’s briefly discuss how teams did.
11 of the 20 teams had a better ‘minutes per clean sheet’ during Project Restart than before it which is barely above average.
However, from traditional ‘Top 6’ teams, 5 teams had a better ‘minutes per clean sheet’ with only Liverpool faring worse than before.
In terms of goals scored, 9 teams did better for ‘minutes per goal scored’ than before. Again, not much of a difference.
Again, Top 6 teams fared relatively better with 4 teams of 6 improving their ‘minutes per goal scored’ compared to before.
In general, this limited data seems to suggest the expectation that having a chance to name 9 substitutes and make 5 substitutions suits stronger teams who are able to utilise their quality squad to greater effect. They tended to score more goals and concede fewer goals compared to a time of 3 subs.
Optimize your squad, check for player stats, and heatmaps on
FFFix
, signup with a 15% exclusive discount!
How did teams use their quota of 5 substitutes?
Data from ‘Project Restart’ shows that teams didn’t use their full quota of subs quite as many times as expected. From 9 games per team, the team using their five subs the most times was Brighton, doing so on 8 occasions. This is in line with a good, versatile squad and a manager who trusts his entire squad and shows tactical flexibility.
Relegation-threated Norwich and Bournemouth both used their full quota of five subs 6-times each, joint third highest.
Liverpool used their full five subs 7 times, Manchester United and Arsenal 5 times, Manchester City surprisingly only 4 times while Chelea did so a measly 2 times. Spurs under Mourinho used their five subs on 0 occasions.
Takeaway from Team substitution data
Fear of five substitutions affecting rotation and player starts was exaggerated compared what transpired in practice. Teams tended to still make subs as needed. Only teams with quality depth would dig deeper into their bench options and even they didn’t do so with the kind of regularity we’d have expected.
How did individual players fare?
Individual player output was a mixed bag with no clear pattern of output emerging. There were also several confounding factors on this data including the effect of no fans in stadiums, games in close proximity and different motivation for teams and players.
A telling takeaway though was that having five subs did not lead to more rotation and hence fewer starts for key players. On the contrary, it seemed like they had a higher likelihood of starting games. Taking title-chasing Manchester City as an example, City would start their ‘strongest’ team and if they had a reasonable lead by the hour mark, they were able to sub off players around the 65th to 75th minute including even centre backs like Laporte. Players were thus able to get their ‘rest’ by seeing reduced minutes per start rather than making fewer starts.
This is a pattern we might expect for players in the Premier League with the new rule change. It is reasonable to expect ‘key’ players to make the same or even more starts compared to before but the likelihood of seeing reduced minutes from a second-half substitution are higher ie lower minutes per start. Compared to defenders, attacking midfielders and wingers seem like the positions where we might see the most number of substitutions so consideration of expected minutes (xMins) for these offensive players in the new reality will be key in evaluating data and adjusting models.

One Underpriced FPL Asset at Each ‘Big Six’ Club | 22/23 Season
2022/23 FPL Pre Season Ultimate Guide
FPL Player Prices
July 13, 2022
July 15, 2022
John Doe FPL
One Underpriced FPL Asset at Each ‘Big Six’ Club | 22/23 Season
In this piece, we will be putting forward one
Underpriced FPL Asset
from all of the ‘Big Six’ Premier League clubs that FPL may have priced too low and could provide great value for your GW1 team. The ‘Big Six’ include Arsenal, Chelsea, Liverpool, Manchester City, Manchester United and Spurs.
Link to all our 2022/23 Pre Season articles
including a transfer tracker of all new signings, all team pre-season friendly updates, team previews, fixture analysis, position-wise analysis, and more. Completely free to access as well!
One
Underpriced FPL Asset
from all of the ‘Big Six’
Arsenal – Gabriel Martinelli (£6m)
Martinelli had his breakthrough campaign in the 2021/22 season and nailed down a spot in Arsenal’s XI after Christmas, in fact he started 19 of his side’s final 25 league games of the season. He has amassed solid underlying numbers, producing
0.47 NPxG+xA/90 in the 21/22 season.
There are question marks surrounding whether he will keep his place in the team or whether Emile Smith Rowe, the player Martinelli replaced on the left side of Arsenal’s attack, will be given the nod at the start of the 22/23 campaign.
Pre-season
will prove crucial in identifying how nailed certain players are, and Martinelli is no different in that respect.
Bukayo Saka is priced at £8m, is more nailed than Martinelli and potentially has penalties but his underlying data last season
(0.46 NPxG+xA/90)
was almost identical to that of the Brazilian’s. If come GW1 Martinelli looks to be first choice on the left of Mikel Arteta’s attack, it will be hard to turn him down at £2m cheaper.
Elite manager team reveals on
FFFix
, signup with a 15% exclusive discount!
Chelsea – Trevoh Chalobah (£5m)
There are a few underpriced assets at Chelsea, especially in defence. Reece James and Ben Chilwell in particular will no doubt provide fantastic value. But Chalobah is a name that is not being thrown around the FPL community very much, and he could have a case for being mentioned more.
Antonio Rudiger and Andreas Christensen have both departed the club and it looks as though Cesar Azpilicueta could be following them soon with a move to Barcelona being touted. This would leave the Blues with just three senior centre-backs in Thiago Silva, Malang Sarr and Chalobah.
Sarr is left-footed and, if coach Thomas Tuchel continues to operate a three-at-the-back system, it is unlikely he will compete with Chalobah for the same spot in the defence. Silva is nearly 38 and will need to be managed so could see fewer minutes than he got last season. Academy graduate Levi Colwill impressed on loan at Huddersfield but, like Sarr, is left-footed so is unlikely to be a roadblock to Chalobah on the right of the back three.
Chelsea will of course make
signings
. Nathan Ake, Jules Kounde, Kalidou Koulibaly, and Matthijs de Ligt have all been linked with the club in recent weeks, but it is worth keeping an eye on their defensive situation. If Chalobah looks nailed, being able to get into one of the best defences in the league for just £5m will provide superb value.
Further Read:
8 Simple & Effective FPL Tips For A Good FPL Overall Rank | FPL Paz
Liverpool – Trent Alexander-Arnold (£7.5m)
It is hard to look past Alexander-Arnold as Liverpool’s most underpriced asset. A defender producing forward-like hauls regularly, he has consistently proven himself as one of the best players in the whole of FPL.
The youngster’s underlying numbers, especially creatively, are worthy of a much higher price. After putting up a career-high
0.36 NPxG+xA/90 in the 2019/20 season
, it was hard to imagine Alexander-Arnold would better it despite his age and potential. But last campaign his figure of
0.44 resulted in his second 200+ point season in his young FPL career
. And still he is priced at just £7.5m.
Of all players to play 1000+ minutes in the Premier League last season, Alexander-Arnold was
1st for xA, 2nd for chances created and 5th for big chances created (all per 90)
. He is a top two creator in the league, playing for a top two attack in the league and top two defence in the league.
FPL’s reluctance to price him higher has meant he is an automatic pick for most people and there is little debate to be had between him and his counterpart Andy Robertson. It would take a brave manager to back against another 200+ point season at just £7.5m.
Further Read:
Manchester City – Ederson (£5.5m)
FPL seem to have priced the Premier League champions pretty accurately as a whole, but goalkeeper Ederson could be a chink in their armour. Dias and Laporte, while both being nailed, come in at a respectable £6m but they offer very little attacking threat so it is hard to justify going for them when Ederson, who is probably the most nailed player in the side, is half a million cheaper.
The likes of Ederson, Alisson and Mendy were all priced at a hefty £6m this time last year and have received price drops to make them more appealing from GW1 due to most engaged managers starting with a £4.5m goalkeeper each season.
Manchester City had comfortably the best defence in the league last campaign, allowing just
27.2 xGA
with their nearest rivals Liverpool on
34.3 xGA
, although both teams conceded 26 goals. Kyle Walker also gives managers a cheap avenue into the City defence at just £5m but with talks of a new full-back being signed it is hard to predict how nailed he is.
Buying Ederson can allow managers to double up on City’s defence while still affording a high-quality outfield. Alongside Cancelo, the pair together cost less than Salah. With Pep Guardiola being synonymous with FPL managers for rotating key players, nicknamed ‘Pep Roulette’, it cannot be understated how precious an accessible and nailed defender in the league’s best backline is.
Further Read:
FPL Fixture Swing Analysis & Best Player Rotation for 22/23 FPL Season
Manchester United – Marcus Rashford (£6.5m)
The Englishman had a hugely disappointing 2021/22 campaign, starting just 13 league games and getting six attacking returns. However with Erik ten Hag now at the helm, there is optimism that the Dutch coach can re-find Rashford’s best form.
With three consecutive seasons of
10+ league goals, including one with 17
, Rashford has proved he can consistently perform in the Premier League. His
NPxG+xA/90 of 0.32 last campaign
was the lowest of his career on record but in the previous four seasons he
averaged 0.46; peaking at 0.54 in 2019/20.
These are not the numbers of a £6.5m midfielder and FPL could end up ruing their pricing if Rashford emulates his best.
The key question with Rashford is whether or not he is nailed. It is a question we do not know the answer to yet and pre-season will prove pivotal in pin-pointing his place in the United squad. Usually, we can make educated guesses if a manager has been at a club for a long enough period of time, but with ten Hag undertaking a new job we are left with a blank sheet.
One thing on Rashford’s side is his versatility. Cristiano Ronaldo has gone AWOL at United and with Edinson Cavani not getting any younger it could pave a way for the MBE to get significant minutes up front.
As with any team United could make signings but as it stands there is little competition for places in the wide positions. Rashford, Sancho, Elanga and Martial can all play out wide but the latter’s career at the club looks to be coming to an end and both Sancho and Elanga can operate comfortably on the right. The spot on the left of United’s attack could be up for grabs and if Rashford can nail it down he could be a steal at £6.5m.
